rulings.Case Title: IN RE: LARRY BLAY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant Everest Denali Insurance Company To Participate In Binding Arbitration And For Sanctions Of $2,500 Against Karoline Kitlowski; Declaration Of David L. Hart
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 1, PETITIONER LARRY BLAY's Motion To Compel Defendant Everest Denali Insurance Company To Participate In Binding Arbitration And For Sanctions Of $2,500 Against Karoline Kitlowski Plaintiff Larry Blay's motion to compel arbitration pursuant to Insurance Code 11580.2(f) and for sanctions is granted in part, denied in part, and continued for further hearing to April 30, 2025. Blay moves to compel arbitration. Defendant Everest Denali Insurance Company does not oppose arbitration but contends that it is premature. The parties have been unable to agree to an arbitrator. The court grants the motion and compels arbitration. Pursuant to CCP 1281.6, the court nominates the following five individuals as potential arbitrators: Hon. David Abbott (ret.) (Judicate West); Thomas H.R. Denver (ADR Services); Hon. Richard S. Flier (ret.) (ADR Services); Hon. Maria-Elena James (ret.) (ADR Services); David J. Stock (ADR Services). The court continues the matter for hearing for selection of the arbitrator to April 30, 2025. If the parties have agreed to an arbitrator from the above list before then, they are to submit a joint filing notifying the court and no appearance will be necessary. If the parties are unable to agree, the court will select an arbitrator from the above list. Defendant contends that arbitration is premature because Plaintiff has recently begun new courses of treatment. Any contentions about the timing of arbitration will be resolved by the arbitrator if the parties are unable to agree. Plaintiff seeks sanctions. The court denies the request. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: BASILIO UDARBE ET AL VS. PREMA P. THEKKEK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. 664.6; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 2, PLAINTIFFS BASILIO UDARBE , INDIVIDUALLY AND ON BEHALF OF ALL OTHER SIMILARLY SITUATED PERSONS, JARMILA ROMANOVA, BRANDON THIBEAULT's Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. 664.6 The unopposed motion to enforce the settlement agreement is granted as specified herein. There is a strong public policy to encourage the voluntary settlement of litigation. (Osumi v. Sutton (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 1355, 1359.) On September 18, 2024, this court entered an order granting preliminary approval of a class action settlement between Prema Thekkek and Plaintiffs. That order was entered upon an unopposed motion for preliminary approval which provided the settlement agreement. (Ex. 1 to Dharap Dec.) The Dharap declaration, and the declaration of settlement administrator Taylor Mitzner attached as Exhibit 3 to the Dharap declaration, establish that Thekkek has failed to carry out the settlement. Thekkek is ordered to provide to Mitzner and Plaintiffs a list of names and addresses of employees of Defendants who enrolled in employer-sponsored insurance coverage between January 1, 2019 and September 18, 2024. Thekkek shall provide the list within 30 days of entry of this order. Thekkek is ordered to meet and confer with Plaintiffs' counsel and the settlement administrator to carry out the settlement agreement. Failure to comply with the court's orders may subject Thekkek to sanctions. The court sets July 17, 2025 for final approval hearing. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: JENNIFER L. WESTMORELAND VS. MATTHEW C. MULLENWEG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination Of Plaintiff
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 5, DEFENDANT MATTHEW MULLENWEG AN INDIVIDUAL, AUDREY HC, LLC Notice And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination Of Plaintiff (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendants Matthew Mullenweg, Kathleen Mullenweg, and Audrey HC, LLC (collectively the Mullenwegs) move to compel plaintiff Jenniffer Westmoreland to submit to an independent medical examination by Hira Hanif, M.D., a forensic psychiatrist. Westmoreland does not object to an IME but seeks limits on what the Mullenwegs proposed. The court grants the motion as set out herein. CCP 2032.310(a) states that a motion for mental examination must "specify the time, place, manner, conditions, scope, and nature of the examination, as well as the identity and the specialty, if any, of the person or persons who will perform the examination. The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer declaration under Section 2016.040." The Mullenwegs have satisfied these requirements. The court may grant the motion "only for good cause shown." (CCP 2032.320(a).) The Mullenwegs and Westmoreland agree that Westmoreland has put her mental state at issue with discovery responses indicating that she suffers ongoing mental and emotional distress. Westmoreland seeks to limit the time for the examination and objected during informal resolution discussions to detailed questioning by the examiner. Westmoreland does not object to the Houston location of the examination. The court finds good cause here. Accordingly, the court grants the motion and orders Westmoreland to attend the in-person examination administered by Hira Hanif, M.D., on April 22, 2025 between 9 a.m. and 5:30 p.m. with a 30-minute lunch break and two additional 15-minute breaks. The examiner shall perform a standard forensic psychiatric evaluation including a mental status examination, which may include questions about medical history, social history, psychiatric history, and substance abuse history. The examiner may administer any or all of the following tests: Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory - 3 (MMPI-3); Test of Memory Malingering (TOMM); Trauma Symptom Inventory - 2 (TSI-2); Structured Interview of Reported Symptoms - 2 (SIRS-2); and Miller Forensic Assessment of Symptoms Test (M-FAST). The examination will also be limited by all of the items that the parties agreed to during their meet and confer efforts, set out at pages numbered 107-109 of Exhibit F to the Nikraftar Declaration, and limitations that are standard to these examinations: No counsel may be present; the doctor will audio record the exam; Westmoreland will not be required to fill out patient information forms; the examiner will not take photographs; Westmoreland will not be questioned about conversations with counsel or her representation; Westmoreland's counsel will receive a detailed report pursuant to CCP 2032.610; and the parties will treat the report as confidential and for use and review by attorneys and retained experts only. The Mullenwegs ask for the examination to be extended beyond 5:30 p.m. if circumstances warrant. The court declines to make the duration of the examination open-ended. In the unlikely event that extraordinary circumstances arise such that the examination cannot be completed within the allotted time, the parties can meet and confer and seek further relief from the court if needed. (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: NKECHI EMERUWA-NEUBERG VS. FRANK LUCCHETTI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Continued Motion And Motion To Compel Ulrich Berg M.D.'S Deposition And Production Of Documents Pursuant To Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 16, DEFENDANT FRANK LUCCHETTI INDIVIDUALLY AND D/B/A LUCCHETTI PROPERTIES'S Motion To Compel Ulrich Berg M.D.'S Deposition And Production Of Documents Pursuant To Subpoenas Defendant Frank Lucchetti's motion to compel production is denied for failure to file a meet and confer declaration. (See CCP 2025.480 ["(a) If a deponent fails to answer any question or to produce any document, electronically stored information, or tangible thing under the deponent's control that is specified in the deposition notice or a deposition subpoena, the party seeking discovery may move the court for an order compelling that answer or production. (b) This motion shall be made no later than 60 days after the completion of the record of the deposition, and shall be accompanied by a meet and confer declaration under section 2016.040"]; Bd. of Registered Nursing v. Superior Ct. of Orange Cnty. (2021) 59 Cal.App.5th 1011, 1031.) The Corman declaration states that the subpoena service issued letters to the nonparty subpoena target and that Defendant's counsel called the nonparty concerning the subpoena (Corman Dec. paras. 2, 5), but that discussion relates to an earlier subpoena whose time to move to compel has expired. No meet-and-confer efforts are recited with respect to the new subpoena as to which Defendant seeks to compel production. Defendant did not act with substantial justification in bringing this motion without an accompanying meet and confer declaration. The court awards $300 in sanctions against Defendant, payable to Plaintiff. (CCP 2023.010(h); CCP 2023.020 [mandating sanctions for failure to meet and confer]; 4/3/25 Drake Decl., para. 4 ["Defense counsel did not make any effort to meet and confer regarding the scope of these records"].) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO JR. VS. COMCAST OF CALIFORNIA III, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Against Comcast; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 7, PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S Motion For Sanctions Against Comcast, Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's motion for sanctions against defendant Comcast of California III is denied, but the court sets an order to show cause re defendants' motion for security. On November 21, 2023, this court entered an order on stipulation between the parties to stay proceedings in this case pending resolution of DiGiacomo v. Recology, Inc., No. CGC-19-581651. On December 19, 2024, the court lifted the stay for the sole purpose of permitting defendants to file motions requiring plaintiff to post security pursuant to CCP 391.1 et seq. Plaintiff's motion to lift the stay was otherwise denied. The only motion permitted to be filed at present in this case is a motion by a defendant to require plaintiff to furnish security. Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is not such a motion and is therefore denied. The court recognizes, however, the potential unfairness in an order that gives defendants the unilateral ability to move the case along or let it sit dormant and stayed. The court therefore orders as follows: defendants are ordered to show cause at hearing on July 14, 2025 why the stay should not be lifted. The court expects that defendants will have ample time to file motions for security before then. If they have failed to file, they may address the OSC through a filing at least nine court days in advance of the hearing and Plaintiff may file a responding brief five court days before the hearing. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. rulings.Case Title: 2075 SUTTER STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION VS. EAST BAY CONSTRUCTION & MANAGEMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 9, PLAINTIFF 2075 SUTTER STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION'S Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint Plaintiff 2075 Sutter Street Homeowners Association's motion for leave to file first amended complaint is granted conditioned on its obtaining a continuance of the trial date to avoid prejudice to newly named defendants. The motion is unopposed. Plaintiff seeks to amend the complaint to state the amount of damages sought. The court finds no prejudice to defendant as the amount is based on repair and reconstruction estimates that were disclosed in expert discovery. (Kim Dec. paras. 15 & 16 & Ex. 3.) This proposed amendment is granted unconditionally. Plaintiff further seeks to amend the complaint to substitute Leonardo Paredes and Emperador Construction, Inc. as Does 1 and 2. The court's concern with this amendment is the proximity to the trial date of May 12. Plaintiff's moving papers establish that it has not unduly delayed naming these defendants; defendant East Bay Construction & Management, LLC (EBCM) was not forthcoming in discovery and Plaintiff did not identify these defendants until recently. (Kim Dec. paras. 9-12.) Nonetheless, a substitution less than a month before trial does not give these defendants time to answer, conduct discovery, or prepare for trial. Accordingly, Plaintiff's motion to substitute these defendants for Does 1 and 2 is conditionally granted on Plaintiff's obtaining a trial continuance, with discovery reopened, of at least six months in Department 206, the master calendar department with authority to set trial dates. Plaintiff is granted leave file the amended complaint substituting the Doe defendants only after obtaining this continuance. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. rulings.Case Title: DEANA HICKS VS. FCA US LLC, A CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorney'S Fees And Costs; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Steven L. Marchbanks In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 10, PLAINTIFF DEANA HICKS' Motion For Attorney'S Fees And Costs, (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff Deana Hicks moves for an order awarding her attorneys' fees and costs against defendant FCA US LLC. Hicks seeks $24,075 in fees, a lodestar multiplier of 1.5, and $650.30 in costs and expenses. FCA agrees that Hicks, as the prevailing party in a Song-Beverly Act lawsuit, is entitled to fees and costs but takes issue with the amount. The court awards $14,005.30 for the reasons stated herein. "A prevailing buyer has the burden of 'showing that the fees incurred were "allowable," were "reasonably necessary to the conduct of the litigation," and were "reasonable in amount." ' " (Nightingale v. Hyundai Motor America (1994) 31 Cal.App.4th 99, 104 [quoting Levy v. Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 807, 816.) The court uses the lodestar method, determining a reasonable number of hours and a reasonable rate. The bills submitted by Hicks's counsel are entitled to a presumption of reasonableness, but upon further review the court concludes that the hours billed are excessive in light of the template nature of Song-Beverly litigation (as evidenced by identical complaints in other cases (Ex. B to Gregg Dec.)). Moreover, Hicks's time records group multiple items in a way that makes it difficult for the court to assess the reasonability of the hours billed (such as when counsel groups review of something with discussion with the client). Other entries are excessive on their face, such as nine hours billed to compiling invoices and completing timesheets or six hours to prepare a reply on the fee motion. With respect to attorney time, the court reduces the 4/16/24 billing entry to one hour, reduces the 4/26/24 billing entry (drafting complaint) to one hour, strikes the 2.5 hour billing for creating discovery plan, strikes the one hour bill for finalizing discovery responses, and awards only two hours for the reply to the fee motion. With those adjustments, the court finds that 18.5 hours of attorney time is reasonable. For paralegal time, the court reduces the complaint-preparation time allowed to one hour and allows one hour for compilation of bills costs, and time sheets. The court finds that the hourly rates sought by Hicks's counsel for his time and his paralegal's time are reasonable. The court awards $12,025 for attorney time and $1330 for paralegal time. The court awards the costs and expenses requested by Hicks, $650.30. The court declines to award a lodestar multiplier in this straightforward action. The total amount awarded in fees and costs is $14,005.30. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: ALEXANDRA VIVEROS VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 17, PLAINTIFF ALEXANDRA VIVEROS' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set One (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff Alexandra Viveros's motion to compel further responses to her request for production of documents, set one, from Ford Motor Company is denied. The court finds that Viveros did not sufficiently meet and confer before bringing this motion. Viveros sent a meet and confer letter to Ford's counsel that attached two sample discovery orders. On February 12, 2025, Ford offered to produce the documents outlined in those sample orders, subjective to a protective order. (Tully Dec. para. 6 & Ex. 5.) On February 18, Viveros returned the protective order. A few days later, Viveros moved to compel without any further efforts to narrow the dispute, after Ford compromised in response to Viveros's letter. When a party fails to meet and confer as required by the discovery statutes, the court must choose between the harsh remedy of denying relief altogether and lesser remedies. (See Obregon v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 424, 435.) Here, however, Viveros's motion fails to explain why Ford's offer to produce the documents in the sample orders Viveros provided was insufficient. Thus, Viveros's motion fails to carry her burden of setting forth specific facts showing good cause for production. (See CCP 2031.310(b)(1).) The court denies the motion. Neither party seeks sanctions, but CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The failure to meet and confer is a misuse of the discovery process and burdens the court with reviewing disputes that the parties could resolve through good-faith efforts. (CCP 2023.010(i).) The court finds that Viveros acted without substantial justification in failing to meet and confer and orders her to pay sanctions of $500 to Ford within 10 court days of entry of this order. (end of tentative ruling part 1 of 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JOSE LUIS RAMIREZ VARGAS ET AL VS. CINDY LEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Defendants Discovery Requests; Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 18, DEFENDANTS CINDY LEUNG AN INDIVIDUAL, JOHN OUYANG' Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Defendants Discovery Requests; Request For Monetary Sanctions Defendants Cindy Leung and John Ouyang's unopposed motion to compel plaintiffs Jose Luis Ramirez Vargas and Leslie Monica Jimenez's responses to its Request for Production of Documents, Set One; Form Interrogatories, Set One; and Special Interrogatories, Set One is granted. Plaintiffs failed to timely respond to this discovery and have thereby waived objections. (CCP 2031.300(a); 2030.290(a).) Plaintiffs are ordered to serve verified responses, without objections, accompanied by responsive documents within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order. Defendants also seek sanctions. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." A comparable provision covers sanctions where a party must move to compel interrogatory responses. (CCP 2030.290(c).) The court does not find that plaintiffs acted with substantial justification and awards $573.57 in sanctions, payable by Plaintiffs, to Defendants within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA VS. BLUE CROSS BLUE SHIELD ASSOCIATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, April 16, 2025, line 15, 1 - PLAINTIFF THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE The motion of Ryan Phair to appear pro hac vice in this matter is granted. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)
